instructions: Copy the .env.example file content to a new .env.local file in the project root directory, then replace all placeholder values (your_firebase_api_key_here, your_project_id, etc.) with actual Firebase credentials from your Firebase Console project settings.

Verify all NEXT_PUBLIC_* environment variables in .env.local match your Firebase project configuration exactly, including API_KEY, AUTH_DOMAIN, PROJECT_ID, STORAGE_BUCKET, MESSAGING_SENDER_ID, APP_ID, and MEASUREMENT_ID - any mismatch will cause authentication failures.

Add .env.local to your .gitignore file to prevent committing sensitive credentials to version control, then restart your Next.js development server with npm run dev to load the new environment variables.

instructions: Copy the UserPreferences type definition from types/UserPreferences.ts into your project's src/types/ directory. Update any existing UserPreferences type imports to reference this new centralized definition to ensure consistency across all modules.
Add the UserPreferences type to your main types index file (src/types/index.ts) by adding the export statement: export type { UserPreferences } from './UserPreferences';

Update all user preference state management (Redux, Context, or Zustand store) to use the new UserPreferences type for proper TypeScript type checking. Replace any inline preference object type definitions with imports from the new types file to maintain a single source of truth.

plan.name: Critical Bug Fixes & Environment Setup
plan.description: Internal development tool to establish proper environment configuration management and fix onboarding logic by creating .env.example template, adding onboardingCompleted flag to UserPreferences type, and updating store logic to use proper onboarding state tracking instead of emotional goal inference.
plan.target_audience: Development team and engineers maintaining the application
features: Generate .env.example with Firebase, Stripe, and Groq variable templates

plan.monetization: N/A - Internal development tool

features: Generate .env.example with Firebase, Stripe, and Groq variable templates
Add onboardingCompleted boolean field to UserPreferences type definition
Update store.ts isOnboarding logic to check onboardingCompleted flag
Type safety improvements for user preferences

instructions: Copy the .env.example file to the root directory of your project, then rename it to .env.local. Replace all placeholder values (your_firebase_api_key_here, your_project.firebaseapp.com, etc.) with actual credentials from your Firebase Console project settings and Stripe Dashboard account.

Verify that all NEXT_PUBLIC_ prefixed environment variables are present in your .env.local file, as these are required by the Next.js application at build time. Missing or incomplete Firebase configuration keys will cause the application to fail during the build process.

After updating .env.local with production credentials, restart your development server by stopping the current process and running npm run dev or yarn dev to ensure the application loads the new environment variables and establishes proper connections to Firebase and Stripe services.
